---
title: "Cameron & Trivedi -- Ch. 10: Nonlinear regression methods (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Colin Cameron
- Pravin Trivedi
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
const DATA = let c = filter(d -> isdir(d) && (isfile(joinpath(d, "auto.dta")) || isdir(joinpath(d, "musr"))), ["../../data", "../data", "data"]); isempty(c) ? error("StatEcon example data not found; run this notebook from its own folder (examples/Cameron_Trivedi) or the repo root.") : first(c) end
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

_rawval(x) = hasproperty(x, :value) ? x.value : x  # unwrap ReadStatTables.LabeledValue
```

The canonical count-data / nonlinear estimators used in this chapter
(`stata_poisson`, `stata_glm`, `stata_gmm_poisson`, `stata_nlcom`,
`stata_lincom`, `stata_estat_ic`, `stata_mfx`, `stata_margins_dydx`,
`stata_margins_at`, `stata_robust_se_glm`) now live in `StatEcon`
(`src/statecon_ch10.jl`) and are called directly below. The chapter-specific
data-loading helper `setup_mus10` and the small display helper
`_print_vce_row` are kept inline as pedagogical scaffolding.

```{julia}
# setup_mus10 — chapter-specific loader kept inline (pedagogical).
#
# Loads mus10data (filtered to year02==1) if `df10` is omitted, drops missings
# on `vars`, fits Poisson via StatEcon.stata_poisson, and returns a NamedTuple
# with both Stata-display order (β, V, X — slopes first, _cons last) and GLM
# order (model, V_robust = fit.V_glm). Any chapter chunk can call
# `s = setup_mus10()` without first running the data-load chunk.
function setup_mus10(df10=nothing;
                    vars=nothing, formula=nothing,
                    vce::Symbol=:robust, quiet::Bool=true)
    if df10 === nothing
        df10 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus10data.dta"))
        for c in DataFrames.names(df10)
            col = df10[!, c]
            T   = eltype(col)
            if T <: Union{Missing, Float64}
                continue
            elseif T <: Union{Missing, Float32, Int}
                df10[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, col)
            else
                df10[!, c] = [ismissing(x) ? missing : Float64(_rawval(x))
                              for x in col]
            end
        end
        df10 = DataFrames.filter(r -> r.year02 == 1, df10)
    end
    cols = vars === nothing ?
        [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income, :age] : vars
    fml  = formula === nothing ?
        GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income) : formula
    df10c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:, cols])
    fit = stata_poisson(df10c, fml; vce = vce, quiet = quiet)
    return (; df = df10c, fit,
              model = fit.model, V_robust = fit.V_glm,
              β = fit.β, V = fit.V, X = fit.X,
              n = fit.n, k = fit.k, coefnames = fit.coefnames,
              coefnames_glm = fit.coefnames_glm)
end
```

# 10 Nonlinear regression methods

## 10.1 Introduction

## 10.2 Nonlinear example: Doctor visits

### 10.2.1 Data description

```{julia}
#=
* Read in dataset, select one year of data, and describe key variables
use musr/mus10data.dta, clear
keep if year02==1
describe docvis private chronic female income
=#
# Read MEPS doctor visits data and keep year 2002
df10 = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus10data.dta"))
for c in names(df10)
    if eltype(df10[!, c]) <: Union{Missing, Float32} || eltype(df10[!, c]) === Float32
        df10[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, df10[!, c])
    end
end
df10 = filter(r -> r.year02 == 1, df10)
DataFrames.describe(df10[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
```

```{julia}
#=
* Summary of key variables
summarize docvis private chronic female income
=#
# Summary statistics
stata_summarize(df10[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
```

### 10.2.2 Poisson model description

## 10.3 Nonlinear regression methods

### 10.3.1 MLE

### 10.3.2 GLM.jl Poisson

```{julia}
# poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust)
m_pois10 = stata_poisson(df10,
    @formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    vce = :robust);
```

```{julia}
# Robust (HC1) sandwich SE computed manually — StatEcon.stata_robust_se_glm
# reproduces the manual computation from the book. It is equivalent to what
# `stata_poisson(...; vce = :robust)` applies internally.
df10c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
pois10 = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                 df10c, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
se_rob = stata_robust_se_glm(pois10)
β = GLM.coef(pois10); cn = GLM.coefnames(pois10)
println("\n— Poisson with robust SE —")
for i in eachindex(β)
    Printf.@printf("%-12s coef=%10.6f  rob.se=%10.6f  z=%7.2f\n",
            cn[i], β[i], se_rob[i], β[i]/se_rob[i])
end
```

### 10.3.3 Postestimation commands

### 10.3.4 NLS

### 10.3.5 The Optim.jl NLS approach

### 10.3.6 GLM

### 10.3.7 The glm command

```{julia}
#=
* Generalized linear models regression for poisson (command glm)
glm docvis private chronic female income,  ///
  family(poisson) link(log) vce(robust) nolog
=#
# glm docvis private chronic female income, family(poisson) link(log) vce(robust)
m_glm10 = stata_glm(df10,
    @formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income);
    family = :poisson,
    link   = :log,
    vce    = :robust);
```

```{julia}
#=
* Command gmm for GMM estimation (nonlinear IV) for Poisson model
gmm (docvis - exp({xb:private chronic female income}+{b0})),   ///
 instruments(firmsize chronic female income) onestep nolog
=#
# Nonlinear IV: E[(docvis - exp(x'β + b0)) · z] = 0 with z = (firmsize,
# chronic, female, income, _cons). Just-identified (5 params = 5 instruments).
m_gmm10 = stata_gmm_poisson(df10, :docvis,
    [:private, :chronic, :female, :income],     # regressors in linear index
    [:firmsize, :chronic, :female, :income];    # instruments
    mean_fn = :exp,
    onestep = true);
```

### 10.3.8 Other estimators

## 10.4 Different estimates of the VCE

### 10.4.1 General framework

### 10.4.2 Standard error options

### 10.4.3 Application

```{julia}
# Different VCE estimates after Poisson regression
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(...))
#
# This whole section (§10.4 — §10.6) shares one fitted Poisson from
# `setup_mus10` (which returns β / V / X in Stata-display order — slopes
# first, _cons last — with the n/(n-1) robust correction).

s10            = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)   # also prints reg table
df10c_v, β_v   = s10.df, s10.β
V_rob, X_v     = s10.V,  s10.X
n_v,    k_v    = s10.n,  s10.k
y_v            = Float64.(df10c_v.docvis)
μ_v             = exp.(X_v * β_v)
u_v             = y_v .- μ_v

# OIM: V = (X' diag(μ) X)⁻¹
V_oim = LinearAlgebra.inv(X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(μ_v) * X_v)
# OPG: V = (X' diag(u²) X)⁻¹  (outer-product of gradients)
M_opg = X_v' * LinearAlgebra.Diagonal(u_v.^2) * X_v
V_opg = LinearAlgebra.inv(M_opg)
# Cluster on age (Stata small-sample correction (G/(G-1)) · ((n-1)/(n-k)))
ages_v = Float64.(df10c_v.age)
S_clu  = zeros(k_v, k_v)
for g in unique(ages_v)
    idx = findall(ages_v .== g)
    s_g = X_v[idx, :]' * u_v[idx]
    S_clu .+= s_g * s_g'
end
G_v      = length(unique(ages_v))
dof_corr = (G_v / (G_v - 1)) * ((n_v - 1) / (n_v - k_v))
V_clu    = V_oim * S_clu * V_oim * dof_corr
# Bootstrap, reps = 400 (Julia's RNG differs from Stata, so this column will
# not match Stata's vce(boot) numbers exactly even with the same seed).
Random.seed!(10101)
B_v = 400
βb  = zeros(B_v, k_v)
for b in 1:B_v
    idx  = StatsBase.sample(1:n_v, n_v; replace = true)
    sub  = df10c_v[idx, :]
    m_b  = GLM.glm(GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income),
                   sub, Distributions.Poisson(), GLM.LogLink())
    βb[b, :] = vcat(GLM.coef(m_b)[2:end], GLM.coef(m_b)[1])
end
V_boot = StatsBase.cov(βb)

ses = (
    VCE_oim  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_oim)),
    VCE_opg  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_opg)),
    VCE_rob  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_rob)),
    VCE_clu  = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_clu)),
    VCE_boot = sqrt.(LinearAlgebra.diag(V_boot)),
)

varnames_v = ["private", "chronic", "female", "income", "_cons"]
colhead_v  = ["VCE_oim", "VCE_opg", "VCE_rob", "VCE_clu", "VCE_boot"]

# _print_vce_row — small display helper kept inline (pedagogical).
function _print_vce_row(label, vals)
    Printf.@printf("%12s |", label)
    for (j, v) in enumerate(vals)
        j == 1 ? Printf.@printf(" %8.4f", v) : Printf.@printf("%11.4f", v)
    end
    println("  ")
end

println("-"^69)
println("    Variable | VCE_oim    VCE_opg    VCE_rob    VCE_clu    VCE_boot  ")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^55)
for (i, v) in enumerate(varnames_v)
    _print_vce_row(v, fill(β_v[i], length(colhead_v)))
    _print_vce_row("", [ses[Symbol(c)][i] for c in colhead_v])
end
println("-"^69)
```

### 10.4.4 Default estimate of the VCE

### 10.4.5 Robust estimate of the VCE

### 10.4.6 Cluster-robust estimate of the VCE

### 10.4.7 HAC estimate of the VCE

### 10.4.8 Bootstrap standard errors

### 10.4.9 Statistical inference

## 10.5 Prediction

### 10.5.1 The predict equivalents

### 10.5.2 Application

```{julia}
# Predicted mean number of doctor visits using predict and predictnl
# (Stata: poisson ..., vce(robust); predict muhat if e(sample), n;
#  predictnl muhat2 = exp(_b[private]·private + ... + _b[_cons]),
#  se(semuhat2); summarize docvis muhat muhat2 semuhat2)

s = setup_mus10(df10)        # self-contained: refits via the setup helper
muhat    = exp.(s.X * s.β)               # = predict, n
muhat2   = copy(muhat)                    # predictnl: exp(linear index)
# Delta-method SE: ∂μ/∂β = μ·x, so SE(μ̂) = μ · sqrt(x' V x)
semuhat2 = [μ * sqrt(x' * s.V * x) for (μ, x) in zip(muhat2, eachrow(s.X))]

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    docvis   = s.df.docvis,
    muhat    = muhat,
    muhat2   = muhat2,
    semuhat2 = semuhat2))
```

### 10.5.3 Out-of-sample prediction

```{julia}
# (Stata: predict resid, score; gen resid2 = docvis - muhat; summarize ...)
#
# For Poisson with log link, the score residual ∂lnL_i/∂(x_iβ) is exactly
# y_i − μ_i, identical to docvis − muhat. Both means are mathematically zero —
# Σ(y − μ) = 0 is the Poisson MLE first-order condition for the constant.

s = setup_mus10(df10)
muhat  = exp.(s.X * s.β)
y_     = Float64.(s.df.docvis)
resid  = y_ .- muhat
resid2 = y_ .- muhat

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(resid = resid, resid2 = resid2))
```

### 10.5.4 Out-of-sample prediction with year01 data

```{julia}
# Out-of-sample prediction for year01 data using year02 estimates
# (Stata: poisson ... if year02==1, vce(robust); keep if year01==1;
#  predict muhatyear01, n; summarize docvis muhatyear01)

s = setup_mus10(df10)        # year02 fit (df10 is already filtered to year02)

# Reload mus10data and subset to year01 to apply year02 coefficients OOS
df10_full = DataFrames.DataFrame(ReadStatTables.readstat("$(DATA)/musr/mus10data.dta"))
for c in names(df10_full)
    if eltype(df10_full[!, c]) <: Union{Missing, Float32} || eltype(df10_full[!, c]) === Float32
        df10_full[!, c] = convert.(Union{Missing, Float64}, df10_full[!, c])
    end
end
df01  = filter(r -> r.year01 == 1, df10_full)
df01c = DataFrames.dropmissing(df01[:, [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
X01   = hcat(Float64.(df01c.private),
             Float64.(df01c.chronic),
             Float64.(df01c.female),
             Float64.(df01c.income),
             ones(DataFrames.nrow(df01c)))
muhatyear01 = exp.(X01 * s.β)

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    docvis      = df01c.docvis,
    muhatyear01 = muhatyear01))
```

### 10.5.5 Prediction at specified regressor values

```{julia}
# Counterfactual prediction: set private = 1 for everyone in year02 sample
# (Stata: keep if year02==1; quietly poisson ..., vce(robust);
#  preserve; replace private = 1; predict muhatpeq1, n;
#  summarize muhatpeq1; restore)

s = setup_mus10(df10)
X_pe1 = hcat(ones(s.n),                         # private = 1 for all
             Float64.(s.df.chronic),
             Float64.(s.df.female),
             Float64.(s.df.income),
             ones(s.n))                         # _cons
muhatpeq1 = exp.(X_pe1 * s.β)

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(muhatpeq1 = muhatpeq1))
```

```{julia}
# Prediction at a specified value of all the regressors via delta method
# (Stata: nlcom exp(_b[_cons] + _b[private]·1 + _b[chronic]·0
#                   + _b[female]·1 + _b[income]·10))
#
# β order is [private, chronic, female, income, _cons], so the regressor
# vector at the chosen point is (1, 0, 1, 10, 1).

s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_nlcom(b -> exp(b[1]*1 + b[2]*0 + b[3]*1 + b[4]*10 + b[5]),
            s.β, s.V; depvar = "docvis")
```

```{julia}
# Linear-combination prediction at a specified value of all regressors,
# reported on the exp scale (Stata: lincom _cons + private·1 + chronic·0
#                                    + female·1 + income·10, eform)
#
# Compared with `nlcom exp(...)`: lincom builds the SE on the *linear*
# combination L = w'β and reports z = L/SE(L); with `eform` the point
# estimate is exp(L), the SE is exp(L)·SE(L), and the CI is the *asymmetric*
# exp(L ± z·SE(L)).

s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_lincom([1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 1.0], s.β, s.V;
             eform = true, depvar = "docvis")
```

### 10.5.6 Prediction of other quantities

```{julia}
#=
* Sample average of predicted number of events
poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust)
margins
=#
# (Stata: poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust); margins)
s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
stata_margins_at(s.β, s.V, s.X)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Sample average prediction at a particular value of one of the regressors
margins, at(private=1)
=#
# Sample average prediction with private = 1 for everyone
s = setup_mus10(df10)
X_pe1 = hcat(ones(s.n), Float64.(s.df.chronic), Float64.(s.df.female),
             Float64.(s.df.income), ones(s.n))
stata_margins_at(s.β, s.V, X_pe1)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Prediction at a specified value of all regressors
margins, at(private=1 chronic=0 female=1 income=10)
=#
# Adjusted prediction at a specified point of all regressors
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_margins_at(s.β, s.V, s.X;
              at        = [1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 1.0],
              at_labels = [:private => 1, :chronic => 0,
                           :female  => 1, :income  => 10])
```

```{julia}
#=
* Sample average prediction at different values of indicator variable private
poisson docvis i.private chronic female income, vce(robust)
margins private
=#
# Sample average prediction at each level of indicator variable private
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_margins_at(s.β, s.V, s.X; over = "private" => 1)
```

## 10.6 Marginal effects

### 10.6.1 Calculus and finite-difference methods

### 10.6.2 ME estimates: AME, MEM, MER

### 10.6.3 Elasticities and semielasticities

### 10.6.4 Simple interpretations

### 10.6.5 Manual marginal-effects helpers

### 10.6.6 MEM: Marginal effect at mean

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effects at mean (MEM) using margins command and finite differences
quietly poisson docvis i.private i.chronic i.female income, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean
=#
# Marginal effects at the mean (MEM)
s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)        # also prints regression table
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2), ("female", 3)],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

#### Comparison of calculus and finite-difference methods

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effects at mean (MEM) using margins command and only calculus method
poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) atmean
=#
# Calculus-method MEM (no `i.` factor prefixes — every regressor is treated
# as continuous, so dy/dx_j = β_j · μ(x̄) for every j).
s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2),
                  ("female",  3), ("income",  4)])
```

### 10.6.7 MER: Marginal effect at representative value

```{julia}
#=
* Marginal effects at representative value (MER) using margins command
poisson docvis i.private i.chronic i.female income, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*) at(private=1 chronic=0 female=1 income=10) noatlegend
=#
# MER — marginal effect at a representative point
s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    factors    = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2), ("female", 3)],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    at         = [1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 10.0, 1.0],
    noatlegend = true)
```

### 10.6.8 AME: Average marginal effect

```{julia}
#=
* Average marginal effects (AME) using margins command and finite differences
quietly poisson docvis i.private i.chronic i.female income, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(*)
=#
# AME — average marginal effects across the sample
s = setup_mus10(df10; quiet = false)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    factors    = [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2), ("female", 3)],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
# Usual ME for a single continuous regressor evaluated at sample means
# (Stata: quietly poisson ..., vce(robust);
#  margins, dydx(income) atmean noatlegend)
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

### 10.6.9 Elasticities and semielasticities

```{julia}
#=
* Elasticity evaluated at mean of regressors
margins, eyex(income) atmean noatlegend
=#
# Elasticity of E[y] w.r.t. income at the sample-mean point
# For Poisson with log link: ε = β_income · x̄_income (independent of μ).
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eyex,
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Semielasticity evaluated at mean of regressors
margins, eydx(income) atmean noatlegend
=#
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eydx,
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Other semielasticity evaluated at mean of regressors
margins, dyex(income) atmean noatlegend
=#
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_margins_dydx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :dyex,
    atmean     = true,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)],
    noatlegend = true)
```

```{julia}
#=
mfx, dydx varlist(income)
=#
# Stata's legacy mfx output (older/textbook form of margins, dydx atmean)
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_mfx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
#=
mfx, eyex varlist(income)
=#
# Stata's legacy mfx with elasticity option
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_mfx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eyex,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
#=
mfx, eydx varlist(income)
=#
# Stata's legacy mfx with semi-elasticity option: ∂lnE[y]/∂x = β_j
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_mfx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :eydx,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

```{julia}
#=
mfx, dyex varlist( income)
=#
# Stata's legacy mfx with dy/ex: β_j · μ · x_j (depends on both μ and x)
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_mfx(s.β, s.V, s.X;
    kind       = :dyex,
    factors    = [],
    continuous = [("income", 4)])
```

### 10.6.10 AME computed manually

```{julia}
#=
* AME computed manually for a single regressor
quietly poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust)
preserve
predict mu0, n
quietly replace income = income + 0.01
predict mu1, n
generate memanual = (mu1-mu0)/0.01
summarize memanual
restore
=#
# AME for income via finite differences: memanual_i = (μ̂(inc+0.01) − μ̂(inc))/0.01
s = setup_mus10(df10)
μ0 = exp.(s.X * s.β)                          # predict, n  (income at original)
X1 = copy(s.X); X1[:, 4] .+= 0.01             # bump income by 0.01
μ1 = exp.(X1 * s.β)                           # predict, n  (after bump)
memanual = (μ1 .- μ0) ./ 0.01

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(memanual = memanual))
```

```{julia}
#=
* AME computed manually for all regressors
global xlist private chronic female income
preserve
predict mu0, n
foreach var of varlist $xlist {
  quietly summarize `var'
  generate delta = r(sd)/1000
  ...
 }
summarize me_*
restore
=#
# AME computed manually for ALL regressors using per-variable step sd(v)/1000.
# These are the *calculus*-method per-obs MEs (≈ β_v · μ_i for log link).
s = setup_mus10(df10)
μ0 = exp.(s.X * s.β)
df_me = DataFrames.DataFrame()
for (nm, idx) in [("private", 1), ("chronic", 2),
                  ("female",  3), ("income",  4)]
    delta = Statistics.std(s.X[:, idx]) / 1000
    X1    = copy(s.X); X1[:, idx] .+= delta
    μ1    = exp.(X1 * s.β)
    df_me[!, "me_$nm"] = (μ1 .- μ0) ./ delta
end

stata_summarize(df_me)
```

### 10.6.11 Polynomial regressors

```{julia}
# AME for a polynomial regressor via the chain rule
# (Stata: gen inc2 = income^2; gen inc3 = income^3;
#  poisson docvis private chronic female income inc2 inc3, vce(robust);
#  predict muhat,n; gen me_income = muhat*(β_inc + 2·β_inc2·income + 3·β_inc3·inc2))
#
# For Poisson with log link: ∂μ/∂income = μ · (β_income + 2·β_inc2·income + 3·β_inc3·income²)

dfp = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
dfp.inc2 = dfp.income .^ 2
dfp.inc3 = dfp.income .^ 3

fit_poly = stata_poisson(dfp,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + inc2 + inc3);
    vce = :robust);

# β order: [private, chronic, female, income, inc2, inc3, _cons]
β_v   = fit_poly.β
X_v   = fit_poly.X
μ_hat = exp.(X_v * β_v)
inc   = X_v[:, 4]

me_income = μ_hat .* (β_v[4] .+ 2 .* β_v[5] .* inc .+ 3 .* β_v[6] .* inc.^2)

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(me_income = me_income))
```

### 10.6.12 Interacted regressors

### 10.6.13 Complex interactions and nonlinearities

```{julia}
#=
* AME for a polynomial regressor: computation using factor variables
quietly poisson docvis private chronic female c.income c.income#c.income c.income#c.income#c.income, vce(robust)
margins, dydx(income)
=#
# AME of `income` from the cubic-in-income Poisson model, with delta-method SE.
# Same point estimate as §10.6.11 but the SE accounts for `income` entering
# the linear index in three terms.

dfp = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
dfp.inc2 = dfp.income .^ 2
dfp.inc3 = dfp.income .^ 3

fit_poly = stata_poisson(dfp,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + inc2 + inc3);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)
β = fit_poly.β; V = fit_poly.V; X = fit_poly.X; n_p = fit_poly.n; k_p = fit_poly.k
inc       = X[:, 4]
μ         = exp.(X * β)
poly_term = β[4] .+ 2 .* β[5] .* inc .+ 3 .* β[6] .* inc.^2
me_i      = μ .* poly_term
ame       = Statistics.mean(me_i)

# Delta-method gradient
g     = vec((μ .* poly_term)' * X) ./ n_p
g[4] += sum(μ)               / n_p
g[5] += sum(μ .* 2 .* inc)   / n_p
g[6] += sum(μ .* 3 .* inc.^2) / n_p
se    = sqrt(g' * V * g)
z     = ame / se
p     = 2 * (1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), abs(z)))
crit  = Distributions.quantile(Distributions.Normal(), 0.975)
lo, hi = ame - crit*se, ame + crit*se

g9 = function(x; w_::Int=10, sig::Int=7)
    sig_use = sig
    s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    cap = (0 < abs(x) < 1 && x < 0) ? 10 : 9
    while length(s) > cap && sig_use > 1
        sig_use -= 1
        s = Printf.@sprintf("%.*g", sig_use, x)
    end
    0 < abs(x) < 1 && (s = replace(s, r"^(-?)0\." => s"\1."))
    lpad(s, w_)
end

println()
Printf.@printf("Average marginal effects                                 Number of obs = %s\n",
               replace(string(n_p), r"(\d)(?=(\d{3})+$)" => s"\1,"))
println("Model VCE: Robust\n")
println("Expression: Predicted number of events, predict()")
println("dy/dx wrt:  income\n")
println("-"^78)
println("             |            Delta-method")
println("             |      dy/dx   std. err.      z    P>|z|     [95% conf. interval]")
println("-"^13, "+", "-"^64)
Printf.@printf("%12s | %s  %s  %s   %s    %s  %s\n",
               "income", g9(ame; w_=10), g9(se; w_=9),
               Printf.@sprintf("%7.2f", z),
               Printf.@sprintf("%.3f", p),
               g9(lo; w_=9), g9(hi; w_=10))
println("-"^78)
```

```{julia}
#=
* Specify model with interacted regressors using factor variables
poisson docvis private chronic i.female c.income i.female#c.income, vce(robust) nolog
=#
# Poisson with interaction female × income — uses `stata_poisson`'s `layout`
# keyword to reproduce Stata's multi-line interaction header.

df_intr = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])

stata_poisson(df_intr,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + female & income);
    vce = :robust,
    layout = [
        "private"  => "private",
        "chronic"  => "chronic",
        "1.female" => "female",
        "income"   => "income",
        :blank,
        ["      female#", "    c.income ", "1"] => "female & income",
        :blank,
        "_cons"    => "(Intercept)",
    ]);
```

```{julia}
#=
* AME with interacted regressors given model specified using factor variables
margins, dydx(female income)
=#
# AME for the interaction model — `stata_margins_dydx` takes an `interactions`
# keyword telling it which X column is the product of which base columns, so
# switching female 0↔1 also rebuilds the `female × income` column and
# ∂μ/∂income picks up the β_intr · female contribution.

df_intr = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
fit_intr = stata_poisson(df_intr,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + female & income);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)

stata_margins_dydx(fit_intr.β, fit_intr.V, fit_intr.X;
    factors      = [("female", 3)],
    continuous   = [("income", 4)],
    interactions = [(col = 5, base = [3, 4])])  # col 5 = female × income
```

```{julia}
#=
* AME computed manually for a complex model
preserve
predict mu0, n
quietly summarize income
generate delta = r(sd)/100
quietly replace income = income + delta
predict mu1, n
generate me_inc = (mu1 - mu0)/delta
summarize me_inc
restore
=#
# AME for income computed manually for the interaction model: bumping `income`
# also has to rebuild the `female × income` column.

df_intr = DataFrames.dropmissing(df10[:,
    [:docvis, :private, :chronic, :female, :income]])
fit_intr = stata_poisson(df_intr,
    GLM.@formula(docvis ~ private + chronic + female + income + female & income);
    vce = :robust, quiet = true)
β_v = fit_intr.β; X_v = fit_intr.X    # cols: 1=priv 2=chr 3=fem 4=inc 5=fem×inc 6=_cons

μ0    = exp.(X_v * β_v)
delta = Statistics.std(X_v[:, 4]) / 100
X1    = copy(X_v)
X1[:, 4] .+= delta                    # bump income
X1[:, 5]  .= X1[:, 3] .* X1[:, 4]     # rebuild female × income
μ1    = exp.(X1 * β_v)
me_inc = (μ1 .- μ0) ./ delta

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(me_inc = me_inc))
```

## 10.7 Model diagnostics

### 10.7.1 Goodness-of-fit measures

```{julia}
#=
* Compute pseudo-R-squared after Poisson regression
quietly poisson docvis private chronic female income, vce(robust)
display "Pseudo-R^2 = " 1 - e(ll)/e(ll_0)
=#
# Pseudo-R² = 1 − ℓ(β̂) / ℓ_0  (McFadden's). `stata_poisson` returns it as
# `.pseudo_r2`.
s = setup_mus10(df10)
Printf.@printf("Pseudo-R^2 = %.7f\n", s.fit.pseudo_r2)
```

### 10.7.2 Information criteria for model comparison

```{julia}
#=
* Report information criteria
estat ic
=#
# AIC / BIC after Poisson regression  (Stata: estat ic)
s = setup_mus10(df10)
stata_estat_ic(s.fit);
```

### 10.7.3 Residuals

```{julia}
#=
* Various residuals after command glm
quietly glm docvis private chronic female income, family(poisson)
predict mu, mu
generate uraw = docvis - mu
predict upearson, pearson
predict udeviance, deviance
predict uanscombe, anscombe
summarize uraw upearson udeviance uanscombe
=#
# Four residual flavors after Poisson GLM:
#   raw      : u_i = y_i − μ̂_i
#   Pearson  : (y_i − μ̂_i) / √μ̂_i
#   Deviance : sign(y_i − μ̂_i) · √(2{y_i·log(y_i/μ̂_i) − (y_i − μ̂_i)})
#   Anscombe : (3/2) · (y_i^(2/3) − μ̂_i^(2/3)) / μ̂_i^(1/6)

s = setup_mus10(df10)
y = Float64.(s.df.docvis)
μ = exp.(s.X * s.β)

uraw     = y .- μ
upearson = (y .- μ) ./ sqrt.(μ)
udev     = [sign(y[i] - μ[i]) *
            sqrt(2 * (y[i] > 0 ? y[i]*log(y[i]/μ[i]) - (y[i] - μ[i])
                                : -(y[i] - μ[i])))
            for i in eachindex(y)]
uans     = (3/2) .* (y.^(2/3) .- μ.^(2/3)) ./ μ.^(1/6)

stata_summarize(DataFrames.DataFrame(
    uraw      = uraw,
    upearson  = upearson,
    udeviance = udev,
    uanscombe = uans))
```

### 10.7.4 Model-specification tests

```{julia}
#=
// Following not presented in book
correlate uraw upearson udeviance uanscombe
=#
# Pairwise correlations of the four residual flavors from the previous chunk
dfres = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    uraw      = uraw,
    upearson  = upearson,
    udeviance = udev,
    uanscombe = uans)
stata_correlate(dfres, [:uraw, :upearson, :udeviance, :uanscombe])
```

## 10.8 Stata resources

## 10.9 Exercises
